data.title: Why Lumentum Wins Over Coherent (For Now)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Late last month, I published a post on Lumentum Holdings (NASDAQ:LITE) advocating my bullish stance on the company after reviewing reports of this hyperscaler advancing their optical roadmap in their data centers. In just 3 weeks since my publication, Lumentum’s shares are up ~50%.

This was also before Lumentum reported their Q2 FY26 ER, which presented an extremely strong reflection on the company’s outlook ahead.

So there must be a strong reason why I am reiterating my bullish views on Lumentum post their Q2 FY26 ER, which I will present in my thesis below.

Additionally, I have noticed many investors are curious to understand the difference between investing in Lumentum vs. its direct peer, Coherent Corp. (NYSE:COHR). I have also explained why I believe Lumentum might outperform Coherent for now below.

Lumentum’s Q2 ER: A Testament To The Rising CPO Adoption Rates

Before I begin my analysis, I will direct investors to my recent analysis on Coherent, which would serve as a good base to compare and contrast my updated outlook on Lumentum post Q2 earnings.

In Q2, Lumentum’s top line came in at $665.5M, continuing its significant acceleration on a y/y basis, growing 65.5%. But the sequential acceleration vs Q1 was outstanding, with Q2 revenues accelerating by a massive 25% q/q. Broadly speaking, Lumentum’s revenues continue to get a boost from rising adoption rates of its optical solutions and components.

Usually, Lumentum has robustly benefited from a sharp uptick in sales of its Components which accounts for 66-70% of Lumentum’s consolidated top line. In Q2, sales of Lumentum’s Components accelerated to 68% y/y vs 64% in Q2. However, Q2 showed that Lumentum’s Systems sales growth has begun to accelerate even faster, growing 60% y/y vs 47% in Q1.

Within the broader theme of optical communication, Lumentum defines its Components segment as sales of foundational, high-performance “building blocks” like optical/laser chips that are supplied to end clients like Nvidia (NVDA), hyperscalers, or other electronics companies to customize their high-speed networking and industrial needs. On the other hand, Systems represents sales of fully fleshed-out optical systems like OCS (Optical Circuit Switches) and advanced optical transceivers that deliver “full functionality to the end customer.”

In Q2 the significant acceleration in Lumentum’s Systems sales not only reflects the rising demand for the company’s OCS product but also an opportunity to expand margins further. That’s because Lumentum is enjoying a monumental surge in orders for its OCS. In the Q2 call, management said this:

Customer demand for OCS is intensifying. Our order backlog now has surged well past $400 million, the majority of which is slated for shipment in the second half of this calendar year. Barring any unforeseen manufacturing or supply chain disruptions, we are well positioned to deliver on this substantial pipeline.

There’s a reason why I highlighted the $400M number above.

Two quarters ago, management revealed that they would be able to surpass the $600M mark in quarterly revenues by Q4 of FY26, ending in ~June. In the Q2 FY26 quarter itself, Lumentum already delivered $665M in quarterly revenues, achieving their $600M quarterly target mark 6 months earlier than expected. The $400M revenue windfall they see from increased shipments of their OCS product gives at least a +10% additional boost to their FY27 revenues, which is front-loaded in H1 of FY27 based on management’s estimates.

By the way, the significant uptick of Lumentum’s outlook of its OCS product is just one part of its acceleration story.

Lumentum’s Components business is seeing +66% growth mostly because sales of its EML (electro-absorption modulator) lasers continue to create new “quarterly company record(s)” in terms of EML laser shipments. Management had already communicated that they are racing to meet incredible demand for EML lasers to such an extent that they are front-loading their capacity plans to fabricate their EML chips. They were supposed to increase their laser chip fabrication capacity by 40% as their FY ends in June. But the significant demand means they might end up creating more capacity, which is “a little bit better than 40%.”

Lumentum’s current capacity expansion plans are well below the +66% growth rates seen in the company’s Component’s sales growth, and that is giving them generational opportunities to price to value and push margins so long as demand stays.

There is a strong reason why Lumentum’s business is benefitting from this surge, which separates the company from its peer, Coherent.

The Difference Between Lumentum & Coherent: Scale-Out

Let me briefly explain the difference between the EML lasers that Lumentum produces vs. the CW (continuous wave) lasers that Coherent produces.

Hands down, Lumentum strongly benefits from the current adoption curve of optical laser chips because many players are on track to start ramping shipments of their scale-out switches some time this year.

As of Q4 last year, industry reports were strongly indicating that hyperscalers like Meta Platforms (META) and Oracle (ORCL) are already moving towards standardizing the connectivity solutions in their respective data centers towards Nvidia’s Ethernet-based Spectrum-X optical scale-out switches.

As Nvidia gets closer towards shipping its Spectrum-X switches, one can expect demand for Lumentum’s EML laser chips to keep exploding higher. Lumentum’s management has been very candid about how scale-out is a huge opportunity for its EML lasers. Plus, their leadership in the EML laser chip market, where they command 50-60% of the market, further accelerates their outlook when it comes to incremental EML shipment growth at a time when scale-out switches are adopting even more EML chips.

This is a very different story as compared to Lumentum’s peer, Coherent, which has a deeper focus on manufacturing CW laser chips.

This is the main reason why Lumentum is able to demonstrate strong 66% top-line growth when Coherent’s revenues are growing at 18%. The strong divergent demand for Lumentum’s EML laser chips vs. Coherent’s CW laser chips are also the reason why Lumentum is enjoying far more aggressive margin expansion paces versus Coherent, as seen below. Note that both Lumentum and Coherent are targeting +40% gross margins, but Lumentum has a clearer sight of achieving the 40% margin targets faster based on the scale of margin expansion Lumentum has demonstrated.

But the adoption of CPO and optical technology in scale-up is still early and away from the 2027-2028 deployment timeline the entire industry is looking forward to. In scale-up the TAM for deployment of optical components like lasers significantly increases because laser chips can be integrated in both scale-up switches and GPUs/XPUs. As Marvell (MRVL) pointed out, CW lasers are generally more “reliable and scalable” while also being “less expensive and easier to make.” There is a general consensus that CW lasers are better suited for scale-up networking opportunities vs. EML laser chips that are better positioned for longer-range scale-out networks.

This key differentiating factor in the optical networking deployment roadmap is the key reason why Lumentum’s EML laser chips are in the middle of a demand boom while Coherent’s CW lasers are still either in stages of being sampled with customers or have moved to initial validation and testing stages.

Therefore, my current view is that Lumentum has stronger prospects as adoption of EML chips in scale-out networks increases, further expanding the outlook for Lumentum, while Coherent’s shares might underperform Lumentum on a relative basis.

Lumentum vs Coherent Valuation

From a valuation standpoint I continue to have a bullish view on both Lumentum and Coherent’s shares. Lumentum’s shares exchange hands at 74x FY26 EPS, expected to grow 267% this year. While this may be expensive, investors must note that Lumentum’s EPS growth estimates were revised up by a massive 29% versus estimates seen before the Q2 ER. Therefore, as long as demand continues to outstrip the 40% capacity growth, investors can expect growth & margin outlooks to be revised higher, leaving enough room for Lumentum’s forward PE to expand further.

On the other hand, Coherent’s shares look less appealing when directly compared to Lumentum’s valuation if I observe Coherent’s forward PE of 41x versus Coherent’s forward EPS growth projections of 51-52% growth. As explained in my recently issued coverage on Coherent earlier this month, I continue to believe Coherent should be valued closer to 45-50x, which still allows for some upside in Coherent’s shares.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

A slowdown in the adoption rates of optical chips and CPO might significantly dent the outlooks of both Lumentum and Coherent. Assuming the demand stays strong, the other risk investors should note is the rising competition from Asian manufacturers who may offer their components at more competitive prices versus the components and solutions offered by Lumentum and Coherent.

Inherently speaking, Lumentum is also looking at ramping up their ambitions to compete with Coherent on CW lasers, as management pointed out last year.

Takeaway

I am bullish on both Lumentum and Coherent, but given the immediate needs of the data center connectivity ecosystem from the perspective of deployment of optical components and solutions, fortune favors Lumentum’s EML chips, which is why I expect Lumentum to outperform Coherent this year.

data.title: Hercules Capital: The Diamond Has Fallen Off A Cliff; Time To Pick It Up
data.description:
This year has not started well for either the BDC sector (BIZD) or Hercules Capital, Inc. (HTGC), which is commonly viewed as a high-quality BDC that should offer better downside protection than the rest of the market.

For HTGC specifically, the first steps this year have been very unpleasant, as the market decided to wipe out almost 20% of the BDC's value in just a ~30-day period starting from mid-Jan:

What is interesting is that in this period there have been no negative announcements or new data points coming out from HTGC.

The only thing that changed and what really caused the massive repricing is the market's sentiment, which became less enthusiastic about software or SaaS businesses (IGV).

The reason for that, as some of you might have already noticed, was and to some extent still is the disruptive power of AI that might impair the business prospects for SaaS companies. Consequently, less ambitious growth expectations for SaaS names have logically led to lower valuation multiples. And here we can include such names as Oracle Corporation (ORCL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Salesforce (CRM), which are large-cap corporations with fortress capital structures.

To put it differently, what has happened is a completely logical equity valuation repricing that adjusts multiples to a new reality or the new consensus that now assumes less lucrative growth.

And here we have to make an important delineation between equity and credit, as the market seemingly has not considered that. The fact that SaaS companies will grow less or some tiny cap businesses that have not gained any meaningful traction in the market fail has very little to do with the system-wide credit risk. The daylight between the consequences from these considerations and HTGC's portfolio is even wider.

Another factor that widens HTGC's distance from the potential justification of the recent selloff is the Q4, 2025 results that got published on February 13.

I will now flesh out my argument why HTGC is a diamond and why it has become a very strong buy as a result of the SaaS-driven drawdown.

Thesis review

To start, let's establish an important baseline in terms of what we have to pay and what we get in yield.

HTGC trades at a P/NAV of 1.28x, which seems high given how the rest of the sector is priced - i.e., at a discount to NAV of around 19%.

However, compared to other internally managed BDCs - Trinity Capital (TRIN), Capital Southwest (CSWC), and Main Street Capital (MAIN) - HTGC's multiple stands well in line with the peers. Actually, given how the gap has narrowed from TRIN and how it has widened from CSWC and MAIN, we can make a clear case that HTGC is relatively cheaply valued.

But, of course, the main driver for my assumption that HTGC is a bargain lies in the underlying fundamentals, not in valuation statistics.

Speaking of yield, the P/NAV adjustment in combination with a stable base dividend and supplemental distributions that have remained intact for 2026 at $0.07 per share (something that we cannot say for "98%+" of other BDCs) have pushed the dividend yield to an attractive 12% level.

There have been only two years when HTGC's average dividend yield hit or exceeded the 12% mark - i.e., 2020, when everything got distorted for obvious reasons, and 2023, when the market got extremely nervous about higher interest rates causing a massive spike in non-accruals (which did not happen).

With this backdrop in mind, I think that it would be worth highlighting several aspects from the recent Q4 report, which, in my view, clearly justify a bullish stance on HTGC.

First, HTGC has once again managed to avoid significant interest rate-driven declines in its NII per share result. In Q4, 2025, the NII per share landed at $0.48, which is $0.01 per share lower than in the prior quarter. Given that we have had 3 interest rate cuts, this can for sure be deemed a positive result.

More importantly, the $0.48 per share of NII generation means that HTGC's base dividend is covered at 120%, which is much better than the sector average, where most BDCs have absolutely zero margin of safety (i.e., exactly at 100%). This kind of coverage significantly derisks the dividend, its stability, and the potential (hypothetical) situation in which HTGC would, for some reason, run out of debt capacity, like, again, most peers have already done.

Second, and this goes directly against the notion of HTGC being a high-credit-risk vehicle, the NAV per share metric increased by 0.7% compared to the previous quarter. On a year-over-year basis, the NAV per share is up by 4% - i.e., a very solid result given a) the attractive dividend package and b) the fact that the lion's share of BDCs have experienced NAV erosion in the same time period.

Part of the reason behind HTGC's NAV expansion has been linked to a record-breaking year of originations and underwritings, which have resulted in a ~20% larger asset base compared to 2024. Yet, a far bigger contributor to this dynamic was the combination of equity realizations and the maintenance of excellent credit quality.

For example, in Q4 alone, HTGC had 4 new M&A events, bringing the total year figure to 15 plus 1 IPO that allowed HTGC to crystalize value from the embedded equity (warrant) co-investments. These all are inherently supportive transactions to organic NAV per share growth.

Third, speaking of portfolio quality, we have to really appreciate that this quarter ended again at a sector-leading result - i.e., non-accrual remained at 0.1% of total portfolio fair value. Plus, to relax the pressure that comes from SaaS risk, it is worth taking into account the conservative approach that HTGC applies to its credit investments. Specifically for SaaS or software lending opportunities, HTGC targets LTVs that are lower than 20% and ARR attachments that are at sub-1x levels. For example, if an HTGC SaaS credit investment company goes belly up, the asset value there would have to be written off by more than 80% for HTGC to actually report a negative consequence to NAV after the full removal of non-accrual.

Finally, I want to make it crystal clear that looking at HTGC and assessing it without digesting the details that lie below the hood, it would be actually quite difficult to find something positive in the context of SaaS-driven selloff.

For instance, the following remark from the CEO in the recent earnings call might not necessarily be the most supportive element in this equation:

Hercules factors this into our technology underwritings, and our focus over the last 12 to 18 months has largely been centered on software companies with a hardware moat or with customer bases that are highly regulated.

Moreover, the fact that HTGC has about 10% of its total income coming from PIK could easily magnify the fears around SaaS-linked non-accruals.

However, as outlined above, HTGC has a very conservative and selective underwriting approach, which manifests itself in sustainable NAV growth, industry-leading non-accruals, and extremely robust leverage and coverage factors (on a weighted average basis) for the current portfolio.

When it comes to PIK, about 86% of these income streams have been structured as such from the get-go, and nearly 91% of them are associated with rating 1-3 companies, which indicates stable credit performance.

The bottom line

HTGC is a unique, internally managed, and conservative BDC, which deserves to be included in durable income investor portfolios.

The market's fears around SaaS and how that would spill over to HTGC credit quality lack a substantiated basis. While I agree that HTGC would deserve a slight repricing - just to, say, account for lower equity-like return potential from its equity/warrant investments - the level of correction has been far too aggressive.

As a result of this pessimism, we now have an opportunity to lock in historically attractive yield, which remains well-supported by resilient and prudent underlying fundamentals.

It is a buy for durable income and stable NAV.

data.title: Alphabet: The Misunderstood CapEx
data.description:
Introduction

The current debate surrounding Alphabet (GOOG) revolves primarily around one number - $175-185 billion dollars in Alphabet's planned capital expenditures for 2026. On one hand, bulls see conviction in Alphabet's spending plan while bears see reckless abandon with no regard for the underlying economics. Both parties share a common trait - they are basing their arguments on the wrong metric. Alphabet's capital expenditures are simply inputs into the equation; the question that truly matters is what type of competitive structure will emerge at the end of this multi-year infrastructure build out and whose architecture is better suited to that new competitive landscape?

Q4 2025

The charts show a significant trend for Alphabet’s overall financial picture over the past six quarters. EBITDA was up at around $42 billion for Q4 2025 versus about $33 billion for Q3 2024 — an approximate 27% increase in eighteen months; that reflects how Google Cloud continued to drive incremental revenue growth while search remained resilient and delivered on the bottom line. EPS also rose from approximately $2.00 in Q4 2024 through Q3 2025 when it peaked at approximately $2.85 in Q3 2025; which indicates that revenue growth was translating into actual earnings per share.

However, the most important indicator is the EBITDA margin line. Although EBITDA margins were near 40.5% for Q1 2025, they have been falling steadily to approximately 38% by Q4 2025 — a 250 basis point decline over the past three quarters even as the absolute dollar amount of EBITDA increased. That is the CapEx thesis in action: the billions of dollars being spent on depreciation ($91.4 billion in 2025), the cost of operating those expanded data centers, and the increasing cost of energy are all eating away at margins as the company scales its top line. The $2.12 EPS in Q4 also supports this — net income per share declined approximately 25% in Q4 relative to Q3 and with flat EBITDA, suggests that other costs (i.e., depreciation, interest from the new debt issuance) are picking up the pace. The key to the bull case is that Alphabet is growing the numerator rapidly, but the denominator is growing faster. The estimated $175 – $185 billion of CapEx spending in 2026 will only make matters worse for the company’s margin pressures before they resolve.

Q1 2026 Outlook

Alphabet begins Q1 of 2026 with increasing revenue growth and unprecedented capex levels. Estimates indicate approximately $106.2 billion in Q1 revenue (a +18% YoY increase) and an estimated $2.59-$2.60 EPS (reduced due to a $175-$185 billion annual capex plan that is almost double the level of 2025). Alphabet set a new high-bar for itself in Q4 2025; it reported revenue of $113.8 billion (up $2.4 billion from estimates), and Google Cloud revenue was up 48% to $17.7 billion with margins at nearly 30.1%, its highest quarterly margin rate ever. Additionally, search revenue grew 17% which represents the quickest quarterly growth rate for search revenue in 2025, proving that the rise of AI will not cannibalize Google’s core business. In addition, two billion monthly users are accessing the AI Overview product and AI Mode is over 100 million MAUs.

Nonetheless, the massive CapEx shock dominated investor views of the quarter. Since 2023, spending has increased roughly six times and accounts for ~46% of projected 2026 revenue.While the $240 billion cloud backlog provides some justification for the large investments being made in AI infrastructure, there are near term limitations to converting this backlog into actual sales due to supply chain constraints.The core tension remains: 18% revenue growth vs. 6% EPS growth, as Alphabet makes a large bet on building out the AI infrastructure needed to support these businesses.

Key Growth Driver

There are no questions as to what the cost curves show. Alphabet was able to reduce Gemini's service costs by 78% over a year through advancements in quantization, speculative decoding, and through hardware-software co-optimization on TPU v5e/v6 clusters. DeepSeek launched R1 at API prices 90-95% lower than OpenAI's o1 utilizing a mixture-of-experts model that only activates 37 billion of its 671 billion total parameters during each forward pass -- an approximate 18 times reduction in the FLOPS/Token compared to a dense equivalent. Epoch AI reported a 280 fold drop in inference cost for GPT-3.5-equivalent performance from the last quarter of 2022 to the last quarter of 2024. With respect to open-weight models, these models are currently within 1-2% of the closed frontier models for MMLU, HumanEval and MATH benchmark scores. These reductions are not incremental but rather exponential reductions in the unit economics of intelligence. And in commodity markets, the greatest profit margin goes to the low cost producer who has the largest market share. And this is Alphabet, and it's not even close.

Custom Silicon as Structural Cost Floor

Google’s TPU program is in its seventh iteration (Ironwood / TPU v7) and was built using a 3nm process with HBM3e memory to deliver more than 7TB/s of peak memory bandwidth per chip. AWS Trainium is currently in its third generation. Microsoft’s Maia 200 shipped in January 2026 and features 216GB of HBM3e memory and over 10 PetaFLOPS at FP4 but is primarily used internally to accelerate workloads. The “maturity gap” matters because designing custom silicon compounds — each subsequent generation learns from the previous generation about architecture, compiler optimizations (XLA for TPUs has been around for nearly a decade) and production yield improvements. SemiAnalysis said TPU v7 was roughly equivalent to Nvidia's Blackwell in terms of raw throughput and offered approximately 44% less total cost of ownership per chip due to Alphabet's vertical integration of the silicon-to-software stack and the elimination of Nvidia's licensing margin.This creates an increasing asymmetry in the cost structures of those providers as inference scales.

Open AI rents the underlying Azure infrastructure -- spending $8.67 billion on inference through Q3 2025 -- and pays an "effective compute tax" in the form of Microsoft's revenue share (reportedly 20% of all API revenue and 75% of all profits until Microsoft recoups its $13 billion investment in Open AI.) Meta purchases Nvidia H100 / B200 GPUs at market pricing and does not have a viable option for custom silicon at a similar level of maturity prior to 2028 at the earliest, based on the current trajectory of its MTIA chip. Anthropic shares its inference load between AWS Trainium and Google TPUs; and therefore inherits the cost floor of the provider it uses. All of these organizations have their margin ceilings determined by someone else's hardware. Alphabet determines its own margins. When inference is costly, having custom silicon is a "nice to have." When inference becomes commoditized, it is a matter of survival -- as the difference between your cost floor and the market clearing price represents your entire gross margin.

Anthropic's commitment to deploying as many as 1M TPU v7 chips – the largest TPU procurement ever made — is an example of how commitments to deploy hardware have been validated through actual capital allocation decisions, and not just through benchmarked comparisons. The fact that Apple has reportedly used Google TPUs to train models gives Anthropic another large, external anchor-tenant using their TPUs. When Anthropic (and others) adopt TPUs in sufficient numbers such that there is enough JAX/PJAX tooling, model libraries, and inference frameworks available that are native to the TPU instruction set, then Google will have built a parallel computing ecosystem that diminishes CUDA's gravitational force on developers. This would give GCP structural "stickiness" — a characteristic that cannot be achieved by IaaS price competition alone.

Distribution as the Terminal Moat

If we can assume that there is some level of convergence among model quality — and it appears as though the Elo ratings on LMSYS Chatbot Arena support such an assumption, as the top six models are separated by fewer than 30 Elo points — then the source of competitive advantage will transition from model performance to surface area of distribution and to data feedback loops. Alphabet's answer: Search receives 8.5 billion daily search queries; 2+ billion active Android devices; 750 million MAUs for Gemini; YouTube serves over 1 billion hours of video per day; 1.8 billion active Gmail users; Google Maps; Google Drive; and Chrome, which currently has a global browser market share of 65%. In contrast to ChatGPT, a destination app with approximately 800-900 million weekly active users, however, exists outside of current workflow patterns. Gemini operates within those workflows -- autocompleting email in Gmail; summarizing documents in Drive; generating code in Android Studio; providing answers to user inquiries directly within Search. The adoption issue is a distribution issue, and Alphabet resolved the same distribution issue a decade ago.

Valuation

Alphabet's stock is undervalued by 29.3%.The one modeling choice that is most important for Alphabet presently is the CapEx path and its return profile. In terms of CapEx, Alphabet is currently forecasting a range of $175-$185 billion in fiscal year 2026, which represents roughly 43-46% of the average of analysts' estimates for the total revenue in fiscal year 2026. This is an amount of CapEx for a company of this size, that has never been seen before. As such, it is expected that near term free cash flow will be significantly reduced before presumably being followed by significant increases in both revenue and operating margins beginning in fiscal year 2027.

When constructing terminal value assumptions for Alphabet, it may also be useful to consider that the CapEx intensity should decrease over time as the buildout of the AI infrastructure matures. Historical CapEx/Revenue ratios for Alphabet were in the 10-12% range; however, the current ratio of CapEx to Revenue for Alphabet was approximately 23% in fiscal year 2025, and is currently forecasted to be 37-40% in fiscal year 2026, which represent peak investment levels. Depreciation & Amortization (D&A) will trail behind CapEx by 3-5 years; therefore, even though gross margins remain strong at approximately 60%, operating margins will be compressed in the near term.

Another key modeling consideration is the "Other Income" line, which transitioned from -$3.5 billion in fiscal year 2022 to +$29.8 billion in fiscal year 2025. The reason for the transition is due to unrealized gains/losses on Alphabet's venture investment portfolio and should be either normalized or excluded from projected operating results. In regards to the Weighted Average Cost of Capital (WACC), Alphabet's relatively low ~1.3% debt weight at market values makes the discount rate equivalent to the cost of equity ~8.9%. As a result, the model will be very sensitive to beta and Expected Return Premium (ERP) assumptions rather than how Alphabet finances itself through the capital structure.

Competition

AWS leads in cloud market share at 28%, however, AWS has a negligible presence in consumer AI — Alexa’s LLM transition has stalled and Amazon does not have a search engine, browser or mobile operating system to distribute AI at scale. The AI strategy for AWS is based upon offering a multi-model marketplace (Bedrock) rather than developing and owning a proprietary frontier model. Alphabet Offers an alternative solution. That is vertically integrated — GCP customers can access Gemini natively through Vertex AI on purpose built infrastructure using TPUs for Google’s own model architecture creating a hardware/model co-optimization loop that bedrock’s model-agnostic approach cannot create.

Risks

Antitrust & Forced Divestiture: In the DOJ's 2026 cross-appeal (and likely subsequent litigation), DOJ will seek structural remedies that may require separating Chrome, and/or Android from the combined distribution platform that is at the heart of our thesis. However, we believe Alphabet's technology architecture is more robust to separation than it might appear. Google's search dominance is derived from its position as the largest query volume recipient and its ability to provide the most relevant search results based on user interaction (relevance feedback loops) and not from default browser settings; Google maintained > 90% search share on Safari and Firefox for years before Chrome was launched. Android's contribution to the AI thesis is as a Gemini distribution vehicle, however Gemini already has 750 million monthly active users (MAUs) via its standalone Search app, Search app within Workspace, etc. Therefore, a potential DOJ requirement to force Chrome to be spun off would create short-term volatility in headlines but would not damage the search relevance feedback loop or the TPU cost advantage that are fundamental drivers of the core thesis. Conversely, spinning off Chrome could create an opportunity to unlock additional value; Chrome as a standalone company would have a high enterprise multiple that the current market assigns no value to when it is included with Alphabet.

Conclusion

In contrast to the traditional consensus view that Alphabet's value is based upon accelerating cloud growth and search resilience, my argument posits that the fundamental investment opportunity associated with Alphabet is a structural one: Alphabet is the only entity capable of controlling all three pillars of its vertically integrated architecture: custom inference silicon (7th generation TPU); frontier foundation models (Gemini); and dominant consumer distribution platforms (Search, Android, YouTube, Gmail). These three pillars are interconnected through a unified data graph that spans the full range of Alphabet's services and enables real-time access to tens of billions of daily user interactions. The vertical integration of Alphabet, from transistor level cost control to billions of daily user interactions, is not a characteristic of Alphabet's business strategy - it is the business strategy.

Moreover, as the market fears, the emergence of commoditized AI capabilities, collapsing inference costs towards zero and decreasing model differentiation, will result in the margin migrating to the party who has the lowest cost floor and the widest distribution funnel. This report explores how Alphabet is uniquely positioned to capture the benefits of such an environment and provides evidence to support the conclusion that the market is underestimating the structural advantage that Alphabet possesses, and therefore the intrinsic value of Alphabet shares, beyond the headline numbers.

data.title: Iron Mountain: Accelerating, Multi-Year Profitable Growth Ahead - Wait For A Dip
data.description:
I previously covered Iron Mountain Incorporated (IRM) in December 2025, discussing why I had reiterated my Buy rating then, thanks to the robust data center related growth prospects and the resilience of its legacy RIM business.

This was especially since the REIT offered a promising dual pronged return prospect across capital appreciation and dividend incomes, thanks to the double digits correction from recent highs, significantly aided by the last dividend raise by +10% and the well covered payouts.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the IRM stock, thanks to the management's multi-year high-growth guidance arising from the Growth Portfolio, the high margin/durable legacy RIM business, and the healthier balance sheet.

This is albeit after a slight correction, given the reduced risk/reward profile, the premium valuations, and the pulled forward upside potential from the recent rally.

IRM Proves Its AI Beneficiary Status

Since my last Buy rating near the December 2025 bottom, IRM has outperformed expectations with an outsized +39% rally as similarly observed in its data center/diversified REIT peers in varying degrees.

Much of their tailwinds are attributed to the ongoing market rotation to value/dividend-oriented sectors, arising from the cooling AI-related sentiments since November 2025, worsened by the increased likelihood of a consecutively paused Fed rate cut in the upcoming FOMC meeting in March 2026.

This is albeit well balanced by the reiterated durability of the AI/data center boom during the ongoing Q4'25 earnings season, as numerous hyperscalers offer outsized FY2026 capex guidance thanks to the tight compute supply environment and the higher pricing trends.

The same has been reported by IRM in the FQ4'25 earnings call, with revenues of $1.84B (+16.4% YoY), richer adj EBITDA margins of 38.2% (inline YoY/+4.5 from FY2019 levels of 33.7%), and AFFO per share of $1.44 (+16.1% YoY).

Much of the tailwinds are attributed to the accelerating data center revenue growth by +37.2% in FQ4'25 and +29.5% in FY2025, despite the tougher comparison from a year ago at +24% YoY and +25.2% YoY, respectively, along with the segment's richer adj EBITDA margins of 51.8% in FY2025 (+6.2 points YoY).

Readers must also note that accelerating growth observed in IRM's Asset Lifecycle Management [ALM] segment, based on the +56% YoY top-line expansion in FQ4'25 and +40% YoY in FY2025 on an organic basis, thanks to the aging data center servers, the consequently accelerated replacement cycle, and the elevated component remarketing revenues.

Demand is likely to continue growing as well, based on the increased "number of Fortune 1000 customers utilizing our ALM services to 360. This is up from 270 in the prior year," aided by the higher cross selling trends observed within its existing customer base.

These reasons are also why IRM's Growth Portfolio, including data center, ALM, and digital segments, increasingly comprise 28% of its FY2025 revenues (+13 points from FY2021 levels), as the management also guides further ratio expansion to ~40% by FY2029.

Combined with the Growth Portfolio expanding at a robust 4Y CAGR of +30% since FY2021 and further expansion expected over the next few years, it is apparent that the REIT is likely to sustain their high growth, profitable prospects ahead:

They accounted for 2/3 of our growth or 8 percentage points of growth on a consolidated basis. This growth portfolio provides an important tailwind in supporting our plan for double-digit top and bottom line growth well into the future, which will only build as the growth portfolio continues to become a larger mix of the overall enterprise. (Seeking Alpha)

These naturally build upon IRM's durable, legacy RIM business, as observed in:

  • the stable Storage Facility Capacity Utilization of 81.3% in FQ4'25 (+1.7 points YoY/-0.3 from FQ4'19 levels of 81.6%),
  • the stable Records Management Retention Rate of 93.3% (+0.7 points YoY/-0.3 from FQ4'19 levels of 93.6%), and
  • the higher Storage Revenue/Sq. Ft. at $11.34 (+12.6% YoY/+49% from FQ4'19 levels of $7.61),

which also explains the segment's richer adj EBITDA margins of 44.7% in FY2025 (+0.1 points YoY/+3.7 from FY2019 levels of 41%).

Thanks to the highly sticky, legacy physical storage business, I concur with the management in which the segment has been able to "drives substantial cash flow and funds growth investments across the business," while triggering their increased "cross-selling opportunity as this is where we originally built our more than 240,000 customer relationships, including 950 of the 1,000 largest global companies."

As a result of IRM's robust, diversified profitable growth prospects across its vertically integrated physical storage/digital storage/asset management capabilities, it is unsurprising that the management has offered the outsized FY2026 guidance, with revenues of $7.7B at the midpoint (+11.5% YoY), adj EBITDA margins of 37.6% (inline YoY), and AFFO per share of $5.74 (+11% YoY).

This is while building upon the FY2025 outperformance by +12.2% YoY, 37.3% (+0.9 points YoY), and +13.8% YoY, respectively, along with the FY2025 FFO per share growth by +15.2% YoY.

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain promising despite the recent downgrades, with IRM expected to report a robust top/bottom-line growth (FFO per share) at +9.5%/+11.8% through FY2028.

This is while accelerating beyond the historical top/bottom-line growth (FFO per share) at a 5Y CAGR of +10.7%/+8.4% and the 10Y CAGR of +8.7%/+5.6%, respectively.

As a result of the accelerated FFO per share growth prospects, it is unsurprising that the market has already re-rated IRM to the current Price/FFO valuations of 27.32x.

This is up notably from the 1Y mean of 25.22x, the 5Y mean of 21.44x, the 10Y mean of 18.21x, and the sector median of 13.93x.

When comparing IRM's Price/FFO valuations of 27.32x and the consensus FFO per share growth estimates at a 3Y CAGR of +11.8% to its data center-related REIT peers, including:

  • pure-play data center - Equinix, Inc. (EQIX) at 30.73x/+10.2%,
  • data center/telecom tower - American Tower Corporation (AMT) at 22.72x/-0.5%, and
  • pure-play data center - Digital Realty Trust (DLR) at 22.80x/+8.8%, respectively,

it is apparent that IRM's recent rally by +39.2% from the December 2025 bottom may have been well deserved, thanks to the double digits FFO per share growth prospects.

This is albeit contributing to IRM's notably lower forward dividend yield of 3.15% against the recent highs of 4.08% in December 2025, with a similar moderation also observed in EQIX at 2.16%/3.30% and DLR at 2.70%/3.30%, respectively, aside from AMT at 3.54%/3.30%.

Otherwise, with IRM still reporting a notably healthy balance sheet at a net debt to adj EBITDA ratio of "~5x for ~2 years," down from FY2019 levels of 5.7x, and a minimal equity dilution by +0.3% YoY in FQ4'25, against:

  • EQIX at 3.8x/+1.5% in FY2025,
  • AMT at 4.9x/inline YoY in FQ3'25,
  • DLR at 4.9x/+3% in FY2025, respectively,

it is apparent IRM remains reasonably valued at current levels against its data center REIT peers, significantly aided by the two recent dividend hikes by +10.1% in November 2025 and +9.8% in February 2025, accelerated compared to the 5Y historical raise by +5.41% and the 10Y historical raise by +5.36%.

If anything, based on IRM's relatively lower forward AFFO payout ratio of 61% compared to the sector median of 73.8%, EQIX at 49.3%, AMT at 63.7%, and DLR at 70.1%, I am of the opinion that the former may be able to offer robust dividend per share raises along the double digits AFFO/FFO growth estimates moving forward:

Our commitment is to continue growing our dividend, building on 4 consecutive years of increases, while we maintain a target payout ratio in the low 60s as a percentage of AFFO per share. (Seeking Alpha)

IRM Remains A Buy Upon A Moderation

For now, IRM has already charted a new 52 week high of $109s by the time of writing, as the stock's nearly vertical upward momentum breaks out of numerous resistance levels and the 50/100/200 day moving averages.

This reason is also why IRM now appears to be somewhat overbought as observed in the elevated RSIs, with it remaining to be seen if the stock is able to keep much of its recent gains.

My caution is attributed to the stock trading at a notable premium against my fair value estimates of $91.50, based on the 1Y Price/FFO mean of 25.22x (nearer to the peer group mean of ~25x) and the FY2025 FFO per share performance of $3.63 (+15.2% YoY).

Based on the consensus FY2028 FFO per share estimates of $5.07 (expanding at a 3Y CAGR of +11.8%), the stock has also pulled forward part of their upside potential to my long-term price target of $127.80, with it apparent that the recent rally by +39.2% from the December 2025 has occurred overly fast and furious.

While I remain highly convinced about IRM's bright long-term prospects across the data center/ALM segments as observed in the management's multi-year high-growth guidance, the high margin/durable legacy RIM business, and the healthier balance sheet, and therefore, reiterating my Buy rating for the stock, it comes with a caveat.

The caveat is that interested investors observe the stock price movement for a little longer before adding, preferably upon a correction to the 50/100 day moving averages of $93s/95s for an improved margin of safety, with those levels also nearer to my fair value estimates of $91.50 while offering an expanded forward dividend yield of ~3.6% nearer to the 4Y mean of 3.76%.

Given the potential -14% downside from current levels of $109s, some patience may be prudent indeed.

data.title: LVS Advisory Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Investors,

For the full year 2025, the LVS Event-Driven Portfolio gained 9.1%, and the LVS Growth Portfolio gained 6.2%, net of all fees and expenses. This compares to the high-yield bond index which gained 8.8%, and the S&P 500 which gained 17.9%, our benchmarks respectively.

In this letter, I will discuss the launch of a levered version of the Event-Driven portfolio, a new strategy for LVS, and I will discuss the 2025 performance of the Growth Portfolio.

Strategy Launch: Levered Event-Driven

When I launched the Event-Driven strategy in 2019, my goal was to provide a low volatility, uncorrelated portfolio that could consistently generate high-single digit annual net returns. 7 years later, I am proud to say that I have accomplished this goal. With a max drawdown of 5.3% and no down years despite steadily outperforming liquid income strategies, the track record speaks for itself.

I’ve long believed that it could make sense to add leverage to “juice up” the returns. I did not pursue this vision for various reasons. Primarily, I knew adding leverage would increase volatility, and I already manage the Growth Portfolio, which is a higher-vol, higher-return strategy. I also lacked the capital base to seed new ideas.

But something changed in 2025. The opportunity set for Merger Arbitrage and Event-Driven situations has improved due to surging M&A volumes, declining interest rates, and a laxer regulatory environment. These factors contributed to the Event-Driven Portfolio’s robust 9.1% net return during 2025, and this this momentum has carried forward into 2026.

At the same time, the US stock market surged to record valuation levels hinged on the success of a narrow set of technology stocks which now account for roughly half of the S&P 500’s overall value. While I am a believer in US stocks over the long run, the current levels of S&P 500 index concentration and valuation make me cautious about allocating fresh capital to the market.

I believe it is a good time to diversify from US stocks and from the S&P 500 specifically.

Within that context, I conducted a study last summer to explore the impact of adding varying degrees of leverage to the Event-Driven Portfolio. I took the actual returns from 7 years of running the strategy and simulated the impact of buying on margin and paying margin interest. While this study is hypothetical, I have a high degree of confidence in it because the daily returns are real and were generated over several volatile regimes of data (high & low interest rate environments, drastic market sells offs in 2020 and 2022, etc.).

The results are extremely promising. A modestly levered version of the strategy can generate returns that would have rivaled the S&P 500 over the past 7 years with less than half of the downside risk. I won’t cite the exact stats here, but I am happy to provide the data upon request.

I secured the backing of a sizable family office, and we went live in October 2025. The strategy is available to accredited investors via separately managed accounts. We are exploring the creation of a hedge fund for the strategy in 2026.

Nothing will change for investors in the unlevered version of the strategy. The levered version of the strategy has the same positions, which means that there is no incremental analytical effort required to manage the additional strategy. A higher-risk strategy isn’t for everyone, and I see value in offering both versions to investors.

Growth Portfolio: 2025 in Review

I have less exciting news to report on the Growth Portfolio. After beating the S&P 500 for the first three quarters of 2025, the strategy lost 8.2% in Q4 which caused us to underperform the benchmark for the year. I am disappointed with how the year ended, but taking a step back and looking at the strategy’s performance over the past 2 years shows that we performed in line with the S&P 500 over a longer period.

The table below shows the key stocks/themes that detracted from our performance during Q4.

LVS Investment

Q4 '25 Impact To Overall Portfolio

Netflix (NFLX)

-4.04%

Power Basket

-1.60%

Software Basket

-1.50%

Fintech Basket

-1.17%

Total

-8.31%

Netflix

Netflix remains our largest investment in the portfolio and was held at an 18% weight at the start of Q4. The stock declined 21.8% in Q4 primarily due to its announced $83 billion acquisition of Warner Brother Discovery (WBD).

As a reminder, we purchased a 6% position in Netflix in 2022 at an average cost of $28.31. Netflix became our largest holding because our thesis was proven correct and the stock price soared (we never added or trimmed after 2022). I kept the stock in our portfolio because I saw more potential upside. Netflix has become the global category killer in scripted entertainment and is rapidly eroding market share from linear TV. I believe the company will continue to take share as it reinvests in live entertainment, sports content, video games, and advertising capabilities.

Prior to the acquisition of Warner Brothers (which surprised us), I perceived the key risks to the thesis to be competition from short form video (TikTok) and user-generated content (YouTube). However, I also view short form video and user-generated content as potential expansion opportunities down the road. In fact, Netflix recently added podcasts to its platform, which is tangential to much of the content that is popular on YouTube. Simply put, Netflix has done an excellent job executing on growth initiatives in the past, and I trust management to navigate future changes in the entertainment industry.

Netflix’s stock is roughly 40% below its mid-2025 levels due to investor concern that the company is overpaying for Warner Brother Discovery. I am neutral on the Warner deal. I don’t believe Netflix needed to acquire a major studio but was simply being opportunistic when Warner went up for sale. Warner boasts one of the strongest content libraries and film production operations in the industry. Netflix could certainly find ways to get more value out of those assets. This is also a once in a generation opportunity to acquire an entertainment asset of this size and quality. I don’t love the price tag or debt associated but believe that Netflix is large enough to absorb the costs. I also believe that owning these content assets will allow Netflix to accelerate its growth plans.

The stock trades for roughly 20x its forward expected earnings. This is incredibly cheap given that earnings are expected to compound at a 20%+ annual rate over the next 3 years without any help from acquisitions. Therefore, I view this as a win/win situation from the current price. Netflix’s stock should perform well with or without the Warner deal over the next 3 years and beyond.

Power Basket

I spent the bulk of the Q3 2025 letter discussing our power thesis. I don’t have much to add. The stocks have been volatile, but I like the longer-term trends.

Software Basket

We held small positions in a handful of software companies. The software industry as a whole has sold off due to fears that artificial intelligence will create competition and reduce switching costs. Like everyone else, our team has spent an inordinate amount of time trying to better understand artificial intelligence and the many impacts it will have on the world. When it comes to software, I have a hard time building confidence regarding what the future will look like. It is hard to argue that AI will increase the terminal value of most public software companies. Given that most software companies started 2025 with relatively robust valuations, any impairment to terminal value would be tough to recover from. I ultimately decided to liquidate our software exposure and move on.

I still see value in owning tech platforms that benefit from physical economies of scale, network effects, and/or brand advantages. Unlike software, which is arguably more commoditized today vs. 3 years ago, platforms should accrue value from AI because they own the customer for reasons that transcend the digital world. AI should allow platforms to better serve their customers and potentially reduce costs.

Fintech Basket

Our fintech basket includes Interactive Brokers and Wise. These stocks are volatile due to potential changes in interest rates and sensitivity to consumer spending activity. Stablecoin risk became a new theme in 2025. I have studied stablecoins and do not see an immediate risk of disruption to our fintech holdings. Wise is more vulnerable given that cross-border pay is considered a strong use case for crypto and stablecoins. To date, stablecoins do not have much traction in cross-border transfer due to much higher costs than traditional payment rails. Wise has continued to post strong financial performance, which gives us some solace that the risk is still theoretical.

However, this risk is worth monitoring closely as technology can quickly change (see the software basket above). I believe Wise still has an opportunity to adopt stablecoin technology and retain its reputation as a disruptor. Wise has an outstanding track record of execution, and I trust management to make the right decisions.

Semiconductors

Semiconductor stocks hurt our relative performance because we did not own them at all in 2025. While I am quite bullish on the utility of artificial intelligence, I stayed away from semiconductors because it is a highly technical sector dominated by specialists. Historically, the industry has been extremely volatile and cyclical, characteristics that I typically try to avoid in investments.

In hindsight, not owning semiconductors hurt us more than anything we owned on a relative basis. Of the S&P 500’s 17.9% return in 2025, 8% (nearly half of the return) can be attributed to the semi industry. In other words, the S&P would have only returned 12% in 2025 if semiconductors had no impact.

I do not see us loading up the portfolio with semi stocks any time soon, but I will be more open-minded to the industry and other industries outside of my typical circle of competence.

What We Got Right in 2025

To end on a positive note, we did get several things right in 2025. Our power basket performed well with Talen Energy doubling and Curtiss-Wright (pitched here) appreciating 64%. Interactive Brokers (pitched here)

continued to compound, appreciating 48%. We also made money on our investment in tech platforms (Google (GOOG), Amazon (AMZN), Meta (META)) and our healthcare basket, which included pharma distributors and medical device companies.

Until Next Time

I am energized to make 2026 a great year. I am excited to expand with the Levered Event-Driven strategy. We also have several promising new investments that I look forward to discussing in the future.

Thank you for your continued support and trust in LVS Advisory.

Best regards,

Luis V. Sanchez CFA

About LVS Advisory

LVS Advisory is a boutique investment firm focused on providing active investment management services for individuals, families, and institutions. The LVS Event-Driven Portfolio is an absolute return strategy focused on capital preservation. The LVS Growth Portfolio is a global equity strategy focused on capital appreciation. Luis V. Sanchez CFA is the Founder and Managing Partner of LVS Advisory. Luis is a licensed Investment Adviser Representative and a CFA Charterholder. LVS Advisory is a Registered Investment Adviser based in Central Florida.

Legal Disclaimer

The information and statistical data contained herein have been obtained from sources, which we believe to be reliable, but in no way are warranted by us to accuracy or completeness. We do not undertake to advise you as to any change in figures or our views.

This is not a solicitation of any order to buy or sell. We, any officer, or any member of their families, may have a position in and may from time to time purchase or sell any of the above mentioned or related securities. Past results are no guarantee of future results.

This report includes candid statements and observations regarding investment strategies, individual securities, and economic and market conditions; however, there is no guarantee that these statements, opinions or forecasts will prove to be correct. These comments may also include the expression of opinions that are speculative in nature and should not be relied upon as statements of fact.

LVS Advisory LLC is committed to communicating with our investment partners as candidly as possible because we believe our investors benefit from understanding our investment philosophy, investment process, stock selection methodology and investor temperament. Our views and opinions include “forward-looking statements” which may or may not be accurate over the long term.

You should not place undue reliance on forward-looking statements, which are current as of the date of this report. We disclaim any obligation to update or alter any forward-looking statements, whether as a result of new information, future events or otherwise. While we believe we have a reasonable basis for our appraisals and we have confidence in our opinions, actual results may differ materially from those we anticipate.

The information provided in this material should not be considered a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any particular security.
