data.title: ImmunityBio: The Story Surrounding Anktiva So Far
data.description:
Thesis

ImmunityBio, Inc. (IBRX) stock has been on a very bullish run. The investor confidence comes from the potential of a chemo‑free immunotherapy platform, which is led by Anktiva. The short idea here is that Anktiva is an IL‑15 superagonist that fuels and sustains NK and T cells without the toxicity we get from chemotherapy or lymphodepletion. The market now has massive expectations, which is where the risk lies. The early clinical data are presented across several cancer types, particularly in recurrent glioblastoma and indolent lymphomas, which show promising immune activation with a manageable safety profile. The main signals investors want to see are potential survival benefits, which can exceed historical norms. Elsewhere, mOS has not yet been reached in glioblastoma, but lymphocyte counts were seen to be restored within one cycle.

Also, keep in mind that Anktiva is already FDA-approved with BCG for non-muscle invasive bladder cancer. It's already generated strong early commercial traction, while broader applications with CAR-NK and antibody combinations still remain investigational. The strategy, however, can't be ignored; it positions ImmunityBio as somewhat of a pioneer in outpatient, immune-directed cancer therapy. I think the market’s premium valuation is well justified despite the limited revenue and early-stage data. So I have to remain buy-rated despite the stock price jumping almost 185% since my previous coverage. I am going to attempt to give an in-depth explanation of why this new therapy platform has such potential and give an overview of the trial progress on Anktiva. Any positive upcoming data on this new therapy approach, I would expect to have a positive stock reaction. With one big upcoming catalyst being earnings. Management is well aware of the potential and the investor optimism; I expect them to focus their discussion mainly on Anktiva and the progress they've made.

Anktiva in combination: why the market is so bullish

A big talking point and one of the main reasons behind the bullish stock response would be the whole concept that ImmunityBio is pursuing here. According to some investors, ImmunityBio’s approach is basically the idea that cancer can be treated without the use of chemotherapy or lymphodepleting drugs. However, we should be careful here to understand exactly what ImmunityBio is trying to do and what kind of data we’ve seen so far. Their approach or mindset is that cancer should be treated by activating/directing the immune system. So, not by first destroying it with chemotherapy or lymphodepleting drugs. Now, traditional CAR-T and many other cell therapies rely on a hefty amount of chemotherapy to essentially clear space in the body so that later, engineered cells can expand. But as you know, that chemo step causes major toxicity, and you can end up with hospitalisations, infections, and a very long road to recovery.

ImmunityBio has instead built its platform around Anktiva, an IL-15 superagonist, and then combined it with natural killer (NK) cells or even engineered immune cells. You see, IL-15 is a cytokine that we usually see in the body being used to grow and sustain NK cells and CD8⁺ T cells. So, Anktiva here is a stabilised, long-acting version designed to massively amplify these cells, but importantly, it can do this without causing the vascular leak and immune suppression we used to see with older cytokines like IL-2. Biologically speaking, this could have huge potential; it could essentially mean that whilst the therapy isn’t replacing the immune system, it can act as feeding it fuel and giving it specific targeting instructions. You can imagine how useful this could be. ImmunityBio has chosen to use it in combinations like CAR-NK + Anktiva + antibody (mainly rituximab), where the CAR or antibody would provide targeting, and Anktiva, with that fueling mechanism, keeps the killer cells alive and numerous enough to matter.

From a patient’s perspective, they obviously wouldn't see any of this. But what they would see is a model that’s designed to be far less physically punishing on them than chemotherapy-based regimens. ImmunityBio wants most of these protocols to be outpatient; that's where one of the real benefits for the patient comes in. They would ideally receive infusions of immune cells (or even antibodies) along with injections/infusions of Anktiva on some kind of a schedule, possibly a 21-day cycle. There would be no preconditioning chemo, so patients would avoid hair loss, marrow suppression, and severe immune collapse. The main sensations they would feel would be the infusion-related effects, such as fatigue, chills, mild fever, or maybe injection-site discomfort. Basically, a reflection of immune activation rather than tissue poisoning. Pain is not a defining feature of the treatment itself, with the discomfort coming mainly from transient immune stimulation, so flu-like symptoms rather than nerve damage or organ toxicity. So you can imagine, this is a pretty massive, fundamental shift. I think of it as, instead of burning down the forest to kill weeds, the therapy is essentially trying to train and energise immune predators already inside the body. That’s why outpatient delivery and repeated dosing become feasible. It’s been a very bullish market reaction, and I expect any positive trial data to come in relation to this new platform to considerably boost the stock further. So it's worth looking into what trial data we’ve seen so far.

What’s been approved so far

Just briefly, before we discuss trial data, it is worth distinguishing what has been approved so far from ImmunityBio and what could potentially come. In terms of approved therapies, Anktiva itself or nogapendekin alfa inbakicept has been approved by the FDA in combination with BCG for the treatment of BCG-unresponsive non-muscle invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC). That's with carcinoma in situ, with or without papillary tumours. Now, this is an FDA-cleared indication, meaning doctors in the U.S. can prescribe it for that specific condition. Anktiva is commercially available in the U.S. and covered by quite a lot of insurance plans. It has also received some regulatory approvals outside the U.S. Approvals include Saudi Arabia for NMIBC. As for the European Medicines Agency, they have issued a positive opinion recommending a conditional marketing authorisation for Anktiva with BCG for NMIBC, which we typically see leading to full EU approval.

For patients who have NMIBC who choose Anktiva, the treatment works as a bladder-directed immunotherapy, not as chemotherapy/radiation. Again, similar to the platform I described above. So in the approved setting from ImmunityBio, Anktiva is given along with BCG directly into the bladder via intravesical therapy for those whose cancer did not respond to BCG alone. The drug is actually delivered via a small catheter placed into the bladder, very similar to how BCG has been given for decades. As you would expect, the procedure is usually done in a urology clinic and realistically takes only minutes. The patient then holds the drug mixture in their bladder for about two hours, then urinates it out. There is no IV chemotherapy involved, no surgery, and importantly, no hospitalisation. In general, people say that it’s not painful, but the catheter insertion can be briefly uncomfortable, and afterwards, patients often feel things like bladder irritation, urgency, or burning with urination for a day or two, very similar to a strong urinary tract infection feeling. Again, we also see the typical mild flu-like symptoms, which inevitibly come with immune activation. So this approach is already a massive contrast to toxicity, no hair loss, nausea, immune collapse, or nerve damage.

The main indication that patients prefer this therapy option would be Anktiva sales, and as you know, ImmunityBio has been recording pretty strong sales. There’s clearly good early commercial traction here since the approval. ImmunityBio reported net product revenue of about $16.5 million in 1Q25 and then $26.4 million in 2Q25. Unit volume growth has exceeded 150% early in the year, and now over 240% by mid-year as adoption expanded. By late 2025, year-to-date sales are reaching roughly $75 million, and preliminary FY25 revenue was reported at about $113 million. That’s about a 700% increase year-over-year. So these figures would indicate that Anktiva is moving beyond just the niche launch phase and establishing a real commercial footprint in its first approved indication. 4Q25 revenue is expected to hit $38.4 million. Earnings are also set to come sometime around March 2nd, and it is another big stock catalyst to look out for. I expect management to speak a good amount about Anktiva’s progress. They know how important the therapy and the overall platform have been for the company. Below is an updated figure from my previous coverage to include the expected results for the next earnings.

I side-tracked a bit, but going back to what's approved for Anktiva, the broader Cancer BioShield platform the company is pursuing as a whole, including combinations with CAR-NK cells, and use in other cancer types such as lymphopenia, glioblastoma, lymphomas, and pancreatic cancer, still remains investigational and is being evaluated. These will be the main drivers of stock growth for investors.

Phase 2 Chemotherapy-Free CAR-NK Cell Therapy

As you know, ImmunityBio has launched a pretty notable Phase 2 trial that’s set to push cellular immunotherapy toward a far less toxic and more accessible model for indolent B-cell non-Hodgkin lymphomas. This would also include Waldenström’s Macroglobulinemia. Again, they’re testing a cancer cell therapy approach designed to work without chemotherapy, without lymphodepletion, and even without hospital stays. So, an off-the-shelf CD19 CAR-NK product is being combined with rituximab and its IL-15 superagonist Anktiva, without using any chemotherapy or lymphodepletion. Again, this is pretty significant because standard CAR-T approaches usually require very intensive preconditioning and inpatient care. Now, ImmunityBio’s regimen here is also designed for outpatient delivery and relies more on innate immune activation rather than general immune system destruction.

The rationale here builds directly on the Phase 1 data we saw, which showed durable complete remissions in heavily pretreated patients treated only with CAR-NK cells plus rituximab. It suggested that an NK-based therapy can actually achieve meaningful tumour control whilst avoiding cytokine release syndrome and the neurotoxicity we usually see with CAR-T. So by adding Anktiva, the goal here is to amplify both NK-cell cytotoxicity and antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity. In doing so, this may overcome resistance to rituximab and improve the depth/durability of the response. Strategically speaking, this kind of trial is testing a platform concept in what ImmunityBio calls Immunotherapy 2.0. In their eyes, this could redefine the treatment for indolent lymphomas as episodic, outpatient immune re-programming rather than continuous chemotherapy or chronic targeted therapy. This would be especially impactful in diseases we consider incurable but slow-growing, so a patient's options are usually long-term toxicity, which takes a massive burden on the quality of life of the patient. Below is a table of all the pipelines surrounding Anktiva.

The glioblastoma program

The glioblastoma (GBM) program from ImmunityBio is also generating notable early signals that have made the market rather bullish. Keep in mind that this is a disease historically defined by very poor outcomes. In the ongoing trial, 23 recurrent GBM patients have been treated so far with a chemo-free regimen combining Anktiva with activated NK and T cells, without lymphodepletion.

So far, 19 of the 23 patients are still alive, and the median overall survival (mOS) has not yet reached. Now, among the 14 patients with evaluable clinical data, the longest survival from time of disease recurrence has reached 12 months, and the mean absolute lymphocyte count rose from a severely lymphopenic baseline of 0.9 x10³/µL to ≥1.4 x10³/µL within one treatment cycle. This measures the number of lymphocytes, a type of white blood cell important for immunity, and normal ALC ranges are roughly 1.0–3.0 x 10³/µL in healthy adults. So after just one cycle of Anktiva with the CAR-NK therapy, the patients’ ALC actually increased to at least 1.4 x10³/µL, which is within the low-normal range. What that tells me is that the therapy is stimulating the immune system, specifically, it’s boosting lymphocytes that are crucial for attacking tumour cells. A good sign. We usually see GBM suppress the immune system, and the standard therapies like radiation and temozolomide don’t help. They further deplete lymphocytes, which just limits the body’s ability to actually fight the tumour itself. So Anktiva, here as IL-15 superagonist, seems to be essentially waking up the lymphocytes and helping them proliferate. That’s what we want to see, and the fact that this happens without chemotherapy or lymphodepletion is a great sign. You can see the ACL count increase in the provided image below.

There has been quite a bit of discussion around the mOS. And I think it is rather significant because recurrent GBM has historically carried a very poor prognosis. The mOS is typically only 6 to 9 months after standard therapy. So the fact that 19 of 23 patients remain alive and the mOS has not yet been reached suggests a pretty meaningful survival signal. Some analysts have modelled the data estimate a conservative mOS figure. Personally, I feel it’s way too early to be attempting models, and we have to keep in mind that any model would be highly preliminary because. Remember, the follow-up is short with a median of about 6 months. So far, only 4 deaths have occurred in the 14 evaluable patients, and the biggest thing is that it is an extremely small sample size, so any estimate is very sensitive to individual outcomes. Also, the survival curve is still pretty immature; the median OS not been reached yet means over 50% of patients are alive, so extrapolation here would rely on assumptions about the shape of the survival curve.

The trial also involves apheresis to collect patient lymphocytes, ex vivo expansion and activation with Anktiva, and reinfusion on an every-two-week schedule for the patients. Again, no chemotherapy or lymphodepletion. As for the safety profile, it’s been pretty manageable, with three treatment-related serious adverse events reported across 41 patients. That 41-patient figure would include both the 23 in the main QUILT-3.078 cohort and also another 18 spIND patients. Importantly, however, there has been no cytokine release syndrome or neurotoxicity observed. So, in having this chemo-free approach, it not only appears to boost the immune competence, but would also suggest a potential big shift in GBM therapy. It's good support for the ongoing development of randomised Phase 2B trials in both first-line and second-line patients.

Valuation is now elevated

The company is currently valued at a $5.86 billion market cap with an enterprise value of $6.46 billion. This is supported by about $257.8 million in cash against a hefty $861 million in debt figure. ImmunityBio trades at an extremely high EV to sales ratio of 57.3x forward after the run-up in stock price. To me, it’s a reflection of aggressive growth expectations relative to their current revenue. Elsewhere, the price-to-sales ratio sits at 51.9x forward. Both of these metrics are well above the sector median, which is more in the 3x to 4x range, which goes to show the premium biotech valuation tag despite being pre‑profit and without meaningful GAAP earnings.

Just to put in context what the stock run-up has done to the valuation profile, in my previous coverage, ImmunityBio was trading at about $2.36 per share. They held back then a market cap of $2.32 billion and an enterprise value of $2.93 billion. On the three-month chart, you can see the investor optimism in the jump in stock price.

The risk

You can probably guess the main risk for ImmunityBio stock here. It's in the extremely high investor optimism we've observed that is priced into the stock. The market is valuing the company at a premium despite only limited revenue and early-stage data, so any negative or underwhelming trial readouts that come, whether in glioblastoma or lymphoma could trigger a sharp sell-off. The overall platform approach surrounding Anktiva is very similar, so negative reads in one setting would mean investor caution in another setting also. The stock’s elevated valuation also makes it particularly sensitive to clinical setbacks.

Looking ahead

Looking ahead, there's a lot for investors to watch out for. Several announcements will be upcoming catalysts, especially the trial readouts on Anktiva and the broader immune‑activation platform. One of the nearest readouts is set to come from the QUILT‑2.005 registrational trial. That's in first‑line, BCG‑naïve non‑muscle invasive bladder cancer, which is now over 85% enrolled. We can expect this by 2Q26, with an interim data look and an eventual BLA submission targeted by the end of the year. We can also expect further progress later in 2026 since the trial matures and potentially yields survival or complete response data that can potentially support broader label expansion.

On the glioblastoma front, full survival curve data from QUILT‑3.078 and longer follow‑up are likely to also come in early 2026. Investors want deeper insight into immune restoration and overall survival signals beyond just the initial median not-yet-reached observations we've seen. In addition to all this, much larger programs like the Phase 3 Anktiva + checkpoint inhibitor trial in non‑small cell lung cancer are enrolling and expected to generate the longer‑term efficacy readouts through to 2027.

I previously tipped that a second Anktiva approval could be the catalyst the stock needs, but now the story has progressed a lot further. The potential upside here seems a lot bigger, and the story is spread out across those several cancer types, but all connected by their approach. The way the company is using Anktiva is what needs validation, and if we see more positive trial data, I expect more upside.

data.title: Earnings Hold The Line As Retailers Get Ready To Report Amid Major Sector Rotation And Tech Fallout
data.description:
Macro Resilience, Tech Resistance as Rotation into Cyclicals Continues

Volatility was once again the theme in US markets last week as a profound alpha rotation reshapes Wall Street. Investors continued to flee mega-cap tech, software stocks, and anything considered an AI loser, in favor of more cyclical sectors such as energy, materials and industrials. The list of impacted industries expanded across certain real estate, trucking and financial services names.

This exodus from software stocks over the last couple of weeks was triggered by advancements in agentic AI, specifically the launch of Anthropic’s (ANTHRO) Claude Code and Claude Cowork. These tools have shifted the narrative from "AI helps software" to "AI replaces software," leading to fears that the traditional per-seat licensing model, the bedrock of companies like Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), is becoming obsolete. As companies realize that a handful of AI agents can do the work of dozens of human employees, the demand for traditional software seats will continue to decline, unless these names can prove they are beneficiaries of AI and show core revenue improvements.

Against this backdrop of tech turbulence, the delayed January Jobs Report provided a confusing but ultimately stabilizing signal for the broader economy. The report showed 130,000 jobs were added in January, more than double the 55,000 expectation by economists surveyed by Dow Jones. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.3% from 4.4%.1 Strength was once again seen in health care, social assistance and construction. Economists are calling this a "low-hire, low-fire" environment. While the labor market isn't exactly thawing, it seems to have found a floor that is preventing a broader economic collapse even as tech workers face a fresh wave of AI-driven layoffs.

More positive macro news came on Friday when the latest Consumer Price Index (CPI) reading was released. The headline annual inflation rate dropped to 2.4% in January (down from 2.7% in December), undershooting the 2.5% forecast and marking its slowest pace since May 2025.2

Q4 2025 Earnings Scorecard - Heading into the Final Peak Weeks

Corporate earnings continued to do their part to support the current economy. With 74% of S&P 500 companies now reporting for Q4 2025, YoY EPS growth stands at 13.2% and revenue growth at 9.0%.3 Consumer-facing names such as McDonald’s (MCD), Coca-Cola (KO) and Marriott (MAR) gave us an early peek at the health of American shoppers ahead of the retail earnings parade which is scheduled to begin next week when Walmart (WMT) reports results. The results from those names were mixed, and pointed to a theme we saw throughout 2025 in which higher-income consumers continue to do the heavy lifting while lower- and middle-income shoppers remain more cautious.

Retail sales results for December also came in flat last week.4 One upcoming tailwind for US consumers? A roughly $100B tax refund windfall is expected in the first half of the year due to permanent individual, estate and business tax extensions in the One Big Beautiful Bill Act (OBBBA). Consumers tend to treat tax refunds as windfall and will most likely spend those discretionary funds.

  • Positive Surprises: 74% of companies have beaten EPS estimates, below the 5-year and 10-year averages. Meanwhile, 73% have beaten on the top line, above both the 5- and 10-year averages.

  • Sector Leaders: Information Technology, Communication Services are leading the charge in top- and bottom-line growth.

  • The Laggards: Meanwhile, Consumer Discretionary is expected to post a YoY decline in EPS, while Energy is estimated to show YoY declines on both the top and bottom line.5

On Deck This Week

This week, the market’s focus will shift from AI and software stocks to the Main Street health of the American consumer. U.S. markets were closed yesterday, Monday, February 16, for Presidents' Day, setting the stage for a shortened but high-stakes four-day trading week dominated by the world’s largest retailer. While Walmart is the headliner, several other names will provide clarity on the sector rotation we've been seeing.

Outlier Earnings Dates This Week

Academic research shows that when a company confirms a quarterly earnings date that is later than when they have historically reported, it’s typically a sign that the company will share bad news on their upcoming call, while moving a release date earlier suggests the opposite.6

This week we get results from a number of large companies on major indexes that have pushed their Q4 2025 earnings dates outside of their historical norms. Six companies within the S&P 500 confirmed outlier earnings dates for this week, five of which are earlier than usual and therefore have positive DateBreaks Factors*. Those names are: Expand Energy Corp. (EXE), Edison International (EIX), Insulet Corp. (PODD), Booking Holdings (BKNG), and Evergy (EVRG). Only Global Payments (GPN) has a negative DateBreaks Factor this week.

* Wall Street Horizon DateBreaks Factor: statistical measurement of how an earnings date (confirmed or revised) compares to the reporting company's 5-year trend for the same quarter. Negative means the earnings date is confirmed to be later than historical average, while Positive is earlier.

Q4 2025 Earnings Wave

In this shortened trading week, 1,033 companies are expected to release results (out of our universe of 11,000+ global names). Currently, February 26 is predicted to be the most active day, with 831 companies anticipated to report. Thus far, 75% of companies have confirmed their earnings date and 41% have reported. The remaining dates are estimated based on historical reporting data.

The Bottom Line

Heading into this shortened trading week, all eyes will turn to the state of the US consumer when Walmart releases Q4 results on Thursday and University of Michigan’s February Consumer Sentiment reading hits the tape on Friday. While the shift toward cyclicals suggests a defensive posture, the combination of a stabilizing labor floor and positive earnings outliers provides a compelling case for optimism. Ultimately, the next four days will reveal whether the current rotation is a temporary retreat or the beginning of a fundamental rebalancing of the 2026 market.

1 The Employment Situation - January 2026, U.S. Department of Labor, Bureau of Labor Statistics, February 11, 2026
2 Consumer Price Index Summary, January 2026, Bureau of Labor Statistics, February 13, 2026
3 FactSet Earnings Insight, John Butters, February 13, 2026
4 Advance Monthly Sales for Retail and Food Services, December 2025, US Census Bureau, February 10, 2026
5 FactSet Earnings Insight, John Butters, February 13, 2026
6 Time Will Tell: Information in the Timing of Scheduled Earnings News, Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis, Eric C. So, Travis L. Johnson, Dec 2018

data.title: RNP: Directly Aligned To Participate In The Growth Of AI Data Centers
data.description:
Overview

When I previously covered Cohen & Steers REIT & Preferred & Income Fund (RNP), I issued a buy rating due to the solid earnings performance and consistent distribution payouts for long-term investors. Now that we've crossed into the new year, we've seen market indices retreat from their all-time highs due to uncertainty and shifting sentiment within the technology sector. However, RNP has provided stability against this uncertainty, and I believe the fund can directly participate in the growth of AI while providing less volatility over time. While it may not be the most efficient holding to get exposure to AI, I believe it is a great fit for investors with a lower risk tolerance.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that RNP's share price has declined by 1.6%. However, RNP has started off 2026 strong and saw its share price increase while the rest of the market indices declined. When including all distributions paid to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 6.2% over the same time frame. RNP now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 7.6% while issuing payouts on a monthly basis. I also anticipate that earnings will improve through 2026 if interest rates continue to trend downward.

Since my last coverage, RNP's price-to-NAV discount has also improved. This disconnect between the fund's price and its underlying NAV may be an opportunity for long-term investors to accumulate. If you are optimistic about the outlook of AI, I believe that RNP can capitalize by providing exposure to the 'picks and shovels,' which provide the essential infrastructure for data centers. While this isn't a main objective for the fund, RNP's holdings directly expose investors to some of the leaders within the space. So let's start by taking a look at the strategy that RNP implements to generate its earnings.

Strategy Benefits From AI

According to the latest fact sheet, RNP has total managed assets of $1.4B that are spread across 298 different positions. The fund has the primary objective of generating current income through a blended portfolio of REITs, preferred, and fixed-income securities. The idea is that this blend allows the fund to participate in the best of both worlds: capital appreciation through bull markets and income generation that can provide a defensive hedge through all market cycles. Since the REITs and preferred securities make up 52% of total assets, I will focus on that part of the portfolio first.

Looking at the fund's breakdown by REITs, we can see that this portion of the portfolio is most heavily focused on health care investments at 18% of total assets. This is followed by an allocation to telecommunications and data center investments, which account for 14% and 12% of assets, respectively. RNP effectively allows shareholders to instantly get exposure to many of the different sub-sectors of real estate.

When looking at the top ten holdings of the fund, we can see that RNP provides exposure to some of the highest quality REITs. Although RNP has exposure to nearly 300 different positions, the fund is a bit top-heavy since its top ten positions make up 29.2% of total assets. For instance, RNP's largest position is Welltower (WELL) at 6.7% of assets. This is followed by many other notable holdings, including American Tower (AMT), Crown Castle (CCI), and Iron Mountain (IRM), just to name a few.

However, I specifically wanted to focus on RNP's allocation to REITs that are expanding their portfolios in some capacity to benefit from the rising demand of data centers. This includes positions like Digital Realty (DLR), Prologis (PLD), and Equinix (EQIX), which all have the capacity to ride this positive growth momentum of the sector. For instance, EQIX recently reported that they expect a 9-10% revenue growth for 2026 as AI-driven bookings accelerate. Additionally, Prologis is apparently in the middle of talks to start an AI data center co-investment fund to capitalize on the current trajectory. Lastly, Digital Realty also anticipates an 8% core FFO growth as hyperscale and AI demand increase.

All of this optimism is being driven by the large-cap companies leading the charge in the AI space. However, the growth of AI is most commonly centered around software at this time. While software is typically seen as a low-cost, high-profit business model, market sentiment is shifting because investors are questioning how the massive capex spending levels will lead to higher earnings growth. As businesses allocate more money to build out the needed infrastructure, funds like RNP are benefitting with their exposure to these REITs. I consider these REITs as the picks and shovels of the AI sector since these data center deals are already settled and many REITs are strategically coming up with favorable terms, such as ensuring that data centers pay their fair share even if power usage varies.

At the close of 2025, the size of the global data center market was estimated to be $39.5B. However, Mordor Intelligence anticipates that the size of the AI data center market will increase to ~$153B by 2031. This represents a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 25.32% through 2031. Even if Mordor Intelligence is half-wrong, this would still result in a large double-digit CAGR over the next few years, which puts RNP in a great position to see meaningful upside in the future.

However, sentiment of the AI center changes quickly, and this is a unique scenario for RNP. The fund has historically focused on real estate securities, which is a much more stable sector over a long period of time. As a hedge against this AI data center exposure, the fund also provides a meaningful allocation to preferred and fixed-income securities. These are positions that put an emphasis on income generation above all else, which can provide stability through times of elevated volatility.

This portion of the portfolio is most heavily allocated to banking securities at 52% of this portion of the portfolio. This is followed by an allocation to Utility and Insurance securities, which both account for 12% of this portion of the portfolio, respectively. This portion of the portfolio doesn't rely on earnings growth, which means that these positions are disconnected from the price movement of the indices.

Just to demonstrate how efficient RNP's portfolio strategy is, we can refer to the performance on a YTD basis. While the S&P 500 (SPY) remains trading sideways, RNP has already set the tone for 2026 and outperformed. RNP's total return is now approaching 9.5%, while SPY has remained flat so far. If sentiment of the tech sector doesn't improve, RNP certainly has a chance to outperform for the rest of the year.

Valuation & Performance Insights

At the time of my last coverage, RNP traded at a premium to NAV of 4.88%. Despite the share price increasing over the last few weeks, RNP now trades at a discount to NAV of 1.96%. For reference, RNP has traded at an average discount to NAV of 0.98% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that RNP doesn't trade at its most attractive price to NAV valuation, but it also sits at a fairly reasonable level. I believe that the fund's disconnect between its price and its NAV presents an opportunity for accumulation at this time.

Unfortunately, the fund hasn't released an updated report that provides transparency into its earnings. So I will periodically check to see if a new annual report for 2025 is released and update my assessment accordingly. However, I still wanted to provide some insight to provide investors with reasonable expectations regarding the fund's outlook and performance throughout the year.

Since the fund puts an emphasis on income generation, this requires RNP to actively sell off the upside growth of its positions. So this means that RNP's best positions aren't able to simply grow without interruption over time. Looking at the long-term performance of the fund, we can see how this structure essentially translates to limited price growth over time. RNP's share price has increased by about 30.7% over the last decade, despite market indices rising to all-time highs. When including all distributions paid out, RNP's total return jumps up to nearly 190% over the same time frame. Therefore, investors should anticipate that most of their returns will come from RNP's ability to maintain a high rate of distributions over time.

Additionally, RNP's focus on REITs and fixed-income securities also introduces a sensitivity to interest rates and leverage. RNP utilizes leverage that currently amounts to 31% of total assets, which is considered to be an aggressive use of leverage in this higher interest rate environment. Despite the Fed initiating several interest rate cuts in 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. So despite the optimism of AI data center growth, the higher interest rate environment may keep RNP's valuation artificially suppressed. A higher cost of leverage can negatively impact the fund's operating spread, and this means that a greater portion of its earnings are being allocated towards debt maintenance.

This complex structure and the focus on income can ultimately lead to underperformance against a more traditional growth- or real estate-focused ETF. So while RNP may not be the most efficient way to capitalize on the outlook of AI data centers, the fund can still be a solid option for investors that want to put an emphasis on income generation.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.136 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 7.6%. If we take this monthly distribution and multiply it by twelve months, we get an estimated annual payout of $1.632 per share. Although the fund hasn't released an updated annual report yet, I wanted to refer to the fund's semi-annual report to demonstrate how well supported the distributions are going forward. For reference, here are the annual earnings amounts over the last few years:

  • 2020: $0.38 per share
  • 2021: $7.11 per share
  • 2022: ($6.44) per share
  • 2023: $2.30 per share
  • 2024: $1.92 per share
  • 2025 (semi-annual): $0.96 per share

Assuming RNP was able to capture a similar level of earnings over the remainder of the 2025 period, the fund is likely to have generated sufficient earnings to cover the distribution for this last year as well. So although the dividend coverage may vary over time, the fund has a history of being able to generate earnings that outpace distributions, which increases the probability that RNP will be able to provide positive NAV growth. Therefore, RNP is a great option for investors that want to collect a reliable stream of dividend income.

Lastly, investors need to consider the fact that the distributions received from RNP may not offer any tax efficiency. Since distributions are supported by a combination of REITs, preferred securities, and fixed income assets, the distributions are primarily classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax consequence for investors. Therefore, a position in RNP would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on RNP at this time. The fund trades at a more attractive price-to-NAV valuation compared to my prior coverage. Additionally, I believe that the fund's exposure to data center REITs directly aligns it to participate in the expansion of AI. The benefit is that RNP's included exposure to income-producing securities means that the fund can provide exposure to AI growth while offering much less volatility compared to traditional equities. This makes RNP a great fit for investors that want to prioritize income generation while also getting exposure to the picks and shovels of the AI revolution.

data.title: Celestica's Next Move Is Scale
data.description:
Investment Thesis

When I began my coverage on Celestica (CLS), I said that CLS looks like a classic picks and shovels winner in AI infrastructure, but of course, with a twist that matters. I also said that in as much as CLS runs in two main segments, the growth engine right now is Connectivity & Cloud Solutions [CCS]. CCS is where hyperscalers and AI builders pull demand forward and then keep pulling as networking speeds step up and rack architectures get built.

In my latest coverage on CLS, I highlighted that what makes CLS well positioned to capitalize on AI is the company's hardware platform and engineering posture. And not just the factory footprint. Celestica has been describing its hardware platform solutions [HPS] as engineering-led, IP-based offerings. They also describe serving data center customers as both EMS and ODM, spanning networking, storage, and racks.

Now, I still believe that the demand curve for data center capacity and power keeps increasing. And Celestica is positioned in the physical layer where that spend turns into shipments. According to McKinsey & Company, data centers could require $6.7Tn in cumulative global capacity outlays in order to keep up with compute demand. And what excites me is the fact that Celestica's own guidance matches that vibe. For instance, management raised 2026 outlook to $17Bn revenue and $8.75 adjusted [EPS] while saying that the 2026 growth trajectory should be sustained into 2027. And this is why capex is being stepped up. In my opinion, if that playbook holds, the stock can still work even after the huge run in 2025. And this is because the business is scaling fast enough to grow into the multiple. I would say that the core risk is not whether AI spending exists. But it is whether Celestica keeps winning and executing in the right parts of the stack without blowing up working capital or capacity timing. And this is why I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Growth Drivers

The core catalyst is not a single quarter. It is the multiyear AI data centre buildout together with networking transitions that forces repeated refresh cycles. I would say that external demand signals still look huge. As I earlier mentioned, according to McKinsey, they expect global data centers may need $6.7Tn of cumulative investment by 2030. Also, the company predicts that capacity demand could more than 3x by 2030, with AI capacity rising sharply as a share of the total. Gartner, on the other hand, separately projects worldwide data center electricity consumption rising from 448TWh in 2025 to 980TWh in 2030. They also expect AI-optimized servers to account for a large share of power consumption by 2030.

Now, I believe that those macro numbers matter for CLS because Celestica is literally planning capex with customers. In Q4 2025, for example, management noted its multi-year capacity view built through close collaboration with key customers and improved long-term demand visibility, as noted by Mandeep Chawla, CFO:

Overall, we are very encouraged by the strong alignment and close collaboration on capacity planning we have with our customers, which underpins our confidence in making these investments.

Secondly, Celestica, as earlier mentioned, raised its 2026 annual outlook. The 2026 revenue is projected to reach $17Bn and $8.75 adjusted EPS while leaving its 7.8% adjusted operating margin outlook and $500Mn free cash flow outlook [FCFs] unchanged. Additionally, the company plans to increase its 2026 capex to about $1Bn, especially with capacity coming online through 2026 and 2027. Some of the highlighted priorities include Dallas-Fort Worth expansion, multiple new buildings at the Thailand campus, investments in Mexico and Japan, and lastly, HPS design centers in Asia and the US.

This is what I think: when a manufacturer raises capex this aggressively while simultaneously raising the revenue and EPS outlook, then it is usually because the demand pipeline looks less optional than the market is pricing.

A third catalyst is product, or rather platform, positioning. I say this because Celestica launched 1.6TbE data center switches built around Broadcom's (AVGO) Tomahawk 6, explicitly calling out OCP ecosystem intent. In my opinion, this is a credible signal that CLS wants to be closer to the platform layer and not just the build-to-print layer.

Valuation

This is where I slow down.

On one hand, Celestica is not cheap on some headline multiples, as shown below. So in this case, I would say that the valuation case will live or die on whether management's 2026 and 2027 guidance holds. As of writing this, the stock was trading at $280 per share. At this price, the stock's FWD Non-GAAP P/E stood at 31x, while its FWD EV/EBITDA stood at 20x and its FWD EV/Sales stood at 1.86x.

And just to make things clearer, the FWD Non-GAAP P/E looks quite rich in comparison to the sector median. I would say that the multiple looks rich if one thinks CLS is still just an EMS name. Nevertheless, it looks more reasonable if one thinks the company is becoming a design-led infrastructure partner for hyperscale AI clusters, with durable program ramps and rising platform content. The company itself supports that narrative in the way it describes HPS, including engineering-led roadmaps and platform solutions across networking and AI compute.

On the other hand, if I look at CLS at a growth-adjusted angle, I note that FWD Non-GAAP PEG stands at 0.72x while the sector median is at 1.48x. This represents a 51% discount relative to the sector median. What this means is that Celestica is trading at a 51% discount to what the average names in the IT sector trade at.

When I step away from sector medians and look at peers, then I note that CLS trades richer than Flex (FLEX), Jabil (JBL), and Sanmina (SANM) on FWD P/E and FWD EV/EBITDA. It also trades cheaper than Fabrinet (FN) on FWD EV/EBITDA, which matters because Fabrinet is often treated as a 'higher quality' optical manufacturer by the market.

Nevertheless, on a growth-adjusted basis, I note that CLS is trading cheaper than most of its peers. At the end of the day, I believe that CLS valuation only works if the company keeps converting demand into revenue while holding margin. And if the capex ramp doesn't create a cash-flow air pocket. The upside case is a durable multi-year hyperscaler infrastructure buildout. On the other side, the downside case is a growth scare at the same moment, and the fixed cost base steps up.

Celestica's Key Risks

Customer concentration is real and getting more important as CCS becomes a bigger share of revenue. In Q4 2025, CLS disclosed that 3 customers individually represented 10% or more of revenue. Now, this means that if even one of those customers pauses a program ramp, inventories and utilization can move the wrong way fast.

I also believe that the capex step-up is a double-edged sword. Management expects $1Bn of 2026 capex and says that additional capacity will come online through 2026 and 2027 with major expansion work across multiple geographies. If the customer roadmap shifts or utilities and power buildouts and commissioning schedules slip, then this means that the return on that capital can lag the narrative.

Final Thoughts

At the end of the day, I believe that Celestica is having a moment, and the market knows it. Still, the numbers suggest that this is more than a momentum trade. As I have elaborated, revenue and margins stepped up through 2025, and management's 2026 outlook implies another leg of growth that is big enough to change what normal looks like for this company.

The part that makes me comfortable staying at a Strong Buy rating is the specificity. This is not a vague 'AI tailwind' pitch. And I say this because Celestica is naming capacity expansions, naming customer-aligned planning, and also describing concrete program awards and production ramp windows into 2026 and 2027.

As of now, my view stays at a Strong Buy rating. And this is because I believe hyperscaler AI infrastructure spend will continue staying elevated. And also Celestica will keep converting that spend into high-volume, high-complexity programs without losing its operating discipline.

data.title: U.S. Coal Markets Quiet As Winter Weather Roils Natural Gas Markets
data.description:
Sufficient inventories and low seaborne demand combined to limit movement of US coal prices during January, even as Winter Storm Fern drove high gas heating and electricity demand in the second half of the month.

Firm natural gas prices are expected to support increased coal generation via gas-to-coal switching, driving stable or growing coal production through 2027. After 2027, the US coal market is forecast to face pressure from the expansion of zero-carbon electricity incentivized in part by broad-based tax credits. Overall, the S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast projects 36.5 gigawatts of coal plant retirements by 2035, despite recent orders to keep coal plants operating.

Coal plant generation share through 2035 is forecast to decline slowly, from 18.3% of total generation in 2026 to 8.4%. The phaseout of tax credits for solar and wind generation is forecast to slow their growth, allowing coal plants to run more after 2030 than previously forecast. The greatest production uplift will occur in the Powder River Basin (PRB), followed by the Illinois Basin (ILB), as reduced growth in wind and solar generation mainly affects the generation mix in the Midcontinental US.

The take

  • After rallying to close 2025, US coal prices held mostly flat in January, restrained by high inventories and low demand in the first two weeks of the year.
  • The S&P Global Market Indicative Power Forecast anticipates 36.5 GW of coal plant retirements by 2035, down slightly from prior forecasts amid market pressure to retain existing generation. Market pressure is attributed to several factors, including high natural gas prices, high demand growth, delays in deploying new gas generation and slower growth of green generation.
  • The production outlook varies by region, with the Powder River Basin and Illinois Basin expecting stable to slightly growing production through 2027, while Appalachian coal production is forecast to decline due to falling domestic demand and limited export growth.

Coal prices were mixed during January, with domestic coal benchmarks mostly unchanged. CAPP region export benchmarks shed $1.00/short ton to $81.00/st (1.2%); NYMEX CAPP added 50 cents/st (0.6%) to $80.50/short ton; and NAPP Pittsburgh Seam 13,000 British thermal units/pound gained $2.50/st to $64.50/st (4.0%). Illinois Basin 11,500 mid-sulfur held at $51.75/st, while the NYMEX Powder River Basin benchmark held at $15.00/st.

Persistent cold weather, including heavy snow and ice, settled across the Eastern US during January, as the reach of Winter Storm Fern extended to states on the Gulf Coast. Natural gas prices soared with heating demand. Henry Hub spot gas prices opened January at a seasonal $4.00/MMBtu and declined to a mid-month low of $2.87/MMBtu before surging to a daily high of $30.72/MMBtu during the final week and closing the month at $10.25/MMBtu. Spot prices averaged $7.55/MMBtu for the month. Storage withdrawals were steady through mid-January ahead of the storm, with working gas at 3,065 billion cubic feet as of Jan. 16 — 177 Bcf above the five-year average and 141 Bcf above the same week of 2025.

The regional gas markets of the upper Midwest and Northeast US, with high gas-heating loads, saw prices rise to substantial premiums above Henry Hub. Chicago Gate averaged $10.21/MMBtu, a $2.66/MMBtu premium to Henry Hub. TCO Pool also moved to a premium of 84 cents/MMBtu for a monthly average of $9.39/MMBtu, while TETCO M3 went to a premium of $10.23/MMBtu for a monthly average of $17.78/MMBtu. With the Western US largely unaffected by the storm, SoCal Border averaged $3.13/MMBtu during the month, $4.42/MMBtu below Henry Hub.

The US Energy Information Administration estimated October 2025 coal stockpiles at 109 million st, growing nearly 4 million st from September. Growing inventories during the fourth quarter of 2025 likely provided a supply cushion for coal plants called on during the January storm.

Current forward pricing for PRB coal has been flat and stable, reflecting sufficient inventories at power plants and mining capacity to increase production as needed against firmer natural gas prices. After 2027, lower natural gas prices and declining coal demand are forecast to restrain price growth.

Bituminous coal prices are primarily influenced by export markets, with today's prices making domestic coal generation generally less competitive against Northeast natural gas. Firmer eastern natural gas prices nevertheless boosted domestic demand through the summer, even as seaborne coal demand declined by an estimated 7.7% from 2024 due to reduced demand from India and China.

Pricing benchmarks exceeding $65/st suggest sustainable returns for eastern bituminous coal, with Atlantic Basin exports above that threshold and coal competing in Pacific Basin export markets generally below it. After declining in 2024, bituminous coal demand for electric generation is expected to remain stable through 2027 on higher electricity demand and more supportive natural gas prices. Declines in steam coal demand are expected to resume after 2027, and overall Eastern US coal demand is forecast to decline 47 million st in 2025–30.

Outlook for US coal production, demand

For the four weeks ending Jan. 24, coal shipments averaged 9.6 million st, 1.7% below 2025 levels. This continues the fourth quarter 2025 trend of elevated inventories, keeping shipments in line with the prior year's levels, with only modest demand uplift. Shipments ended 2025 at an aggregate 535 million st, implying an overall inventory drawdown from year-end 2025 between 5 and 10 million st.

The chart below compares the current production forecast with recent history. We forecast increased coal demand against higher natural gas prices through 2027, with annual tonnage at 550 million st. Beginning in 2028, relative normalization of coal and gas pricing and growth in green generation is forecast to pressure coal generation lower. The overall coal market, including domestic demand and exports, is forecast to decline by 140 million st between 2026 and 2031.

Production outlook — Powder River Basin

Production reports of the Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA) through the third quarter of 2025 indicate production of 172.2 million st, an annualized rate of 229.6 million st. Production is now forecast at 230 million st in 2025, with higher inventories potentially constraining fourth quarter production. Production is forecast to grow through 2027 against higher natural gas prices. By 2030, S&P Global Energy projects that coal retirements in the Midwest and expansion of wind generation in PRB's core markets will gradually shrink the coal demand to 204 million st, declining further to 177 million st through 2035.

Production outlook — Illinois Basin

MSHA's 2025 September quarter production reports indicate production of 50.6 million st, or an annualized rate of 67.5 million st. We forecast production will grow from 68 million st in 2025 to an annual 75 million st through 2027, after which the expansion of wind generation and announced coal retirements are forecast to erode ILB coal demand. Coal production in the ILB is forecast to fall to 65 million st by 2030, declining further to 50 million st by 2035.

Production outlook — Appalachian basins

MSHA's production reports for the year's third quarter indicate year-to-date production at 118.8 million st, or an annualized rate of 158.4 million st. Appalachian coal demand tends to be more sensitive to global seaborne markets than to domestic natural gas prices, unlike the PRB or the ILB, which are forecast for improved demand against natural gas generation. Therefore, gains in Appalachian coal will be more limited. We forecast 2025 production at 159 million st, just 0.6% higher than 2024. As remaining domestic demand erodes after 2027, with only modest offsets from export growth, Appalachian production is forecast to fall to 112 million st by 2030.
